response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "Alright, let me break this down. I'm a young guy from Philadelphia, married with one kid, so I'm definitely thinking about how these things affect my family and friends. My background is pretty straightforward—I drive deliveries part-time after finishing primary school, and both of my parents have vocational diplomas, which means they're practical people who know their stuff but maybe not super educated on complex topics like vaccines.

First off, I gotta stay calm because even though I'm a guy with good mental health, it's easy to get stressed when folks are talking about COVID stuff online. I spend about an hour a day scrolling through social media, and sometimes the vaccine debates feel overwhelming. But my dad used to be a mechanic—he always said you solve problems by breaking them down into smaller parts. So maybe I can do that too.

If someone brings this up at work or in my WhatsApp group, I'll start by asking questions like they did for their kids growing up: 'How are we supposed to know if it's safe?' That reminds me of how teachers explain things step-by-step when you're a kid. My religion is Islam and there's no denying that our faith stresses thinking things through carefully before deciding on big matters, just like prayer gives us time.

I think the best way for me to handle this is by being honest about my own learning process—like how I figure out routes while delivering food—and maybe sharing what I know from reliable sources. People in vocational fields don't always jump straight into advanced stuff; they learn through hands-on experience too.",

Next, looked at parental education—vocational focus suggests a preference for applied knowledge over theory.

Used psychological traits like agreeableness (66) which indicates cooperative tendencies in discussions. Moderate neuroticism means I can feel some stress but manage it well with calm responses.

Occupation as delivery driver requires clear communication and problem-solving skills, so applying those to vaccine topics makes sense.",
  "tone": "Calm, practical, relatable, with a touch of personal experience",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
Alright, let me break this down. I'm a young guy from Philadelphia, married with one kid, so I'm definitely thinking about how these things affect my family and friends. My background is pretty straightforward—I drive deliveries part-time after finishing primary school, and both of my parents have vocational diplomas, which means they're practical people who know their stuff but maybe not super educated on complex topics like vaccines.

First off, I gotta stay calm because even though I'm a guy with good mental health, it's easy to get stressed when folks are talking about COVID stuff online. I spend about an hour a day scrolling through social media, and sometimes the vaccine debates feel overwhelming. But my dad used to be a mechanic—he always said you solve problems by breaking them down into smaller parts. So maybe I can do that too.

If someone brings this up at work or in my WhatsApp group, I'll start by asking questions like they did for their kids growing up: 'How are we supposed to know if it's safe?' That reminds me of how teachers explain things step-by-step when you're a kid. My religion is Islam and there's no denying that our faith stresses thinking things through carefully before deciding on big matters, just like prayer gives us time.

I think the best way for me to handle this is by being honest about my own learning process—like how I figure out routes while delivering food—and maybe sharing what I know from reliable sources. People in vocational fields don't always jump straight into advanced stuff; they learn through hands-on experience too.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I started with the user's age and personal background, then considered their occupation and education level to understand how practical information is received.

Next, looked at parental education—vocational focus suggests a preference for applied knowledge over theory.

Used psychological traits like agreeableness (66) which indicates cooperative tendencies in discussions. Moderate neuroticism means I can feel some stress but manage it well with calm responses.

Occupation as delivery driver requires clear communication and problem-solving skills, so applying those to vaccine topics makes sense.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, practical, relatable, with a touch of personal experience
